responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence  

It is with great pleasure that I write this editorial to a new Special Issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy on the topic of eating disorders, a subject that was previously addressed in volume 18, no. 4, in 2004.

All the papers in this issue are based on work written in connection with the MA course, Working with people with eating disorders, delivered at the Tavistock Clinic in collaboration with the University of East London.

The students who study on this programme mostly work as clinicians in specialist eating disorder units. They are nurses, psychologists, dieticians and occasionally psychotherapists. Our team at the Tavistock comprises Gianna Williams, Roberta Mondadori, Selina Perocevic and myself; adult, adolescent, child and infant psychotherapists.

Our Tavistock course does not seek to ‘convert’ our students to psychoanalytic psychotherapy. We just try to use our psychoanalytic understanding to help the students to make sense of the very difficult work they do. This Issue, I hope, shows that we are ‘on task’. The papers speak for themselves.

Many thanks to Geraldine Shipton for her commitment to this project and for her work with me on this issue.

responseSet.title: The interplay between biological and psychological factors in determining vulnerability to mental disorders
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The interplay between biological and psychological factors in determining vulnerability to mental disorders

David Goldberg

David Goldberg

David Goldberg  

Maternal attachment, Gene/environment interactions, HPA axis, Hippocampus

This paper reviews recent research with animals which has cast new light on the mechanism and consequences of maternal attachment, and goes on to describe research in humans which has investigated the extent to which the same mechanisms may apply to us. It is clear that while some features of the adult are mediated by genes, environmental factors – notably good maternal attachment, are also of great importance. Stress during pregnancy can interfere with good maternal care with effects upon the hippocampus and the sensitivity of the infant's hypothalamo-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis. Interactions between gene expression and environment, between behaviour and genotype are important in the way they provide explanations of how the many different features that make-up the ‘depressive diathesis’ arise.

responseSet.title: Consenting to be robbed so as not to be murdered
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Consenting to be robbed so as not to be murdered

David Taylor

David Taylor

David Taylor  

psychoanalysis, chronic depression, compromise equilibrium, subjectivity

This paper first addresses the place of psychoanalysis in relation to certain other key bodies of knowledge: the thesis is that psychoanalysis is best understood as a highly specialized branch of human biology. Within this framework the main psychoanalytic ideas about depression in adults are described, giving particular attention to depression when it becomes a chronic, treatment-resistant state. Goldberg shows (pages 236–247 this issue) that the advances in genetics and neurobiology can be connected with those in developmental research (Murray & Hill, pages 185–199 & 200–212 this issue). This enables new light to be thrown upon the psychobiological diathesis which seems to underlie depression. This paper argues that an investigation of subjectivity and meaning is also necessary if our account of depression is to be complete. Without an understanding of meaningfulness, the function of the brain cannot be fully known. Finally, some clinical material shows why psychic reality has to be approached in its own terms.

[[RunningHead]]

The development of children of postnatally depressed mothers: Evidence from the Cambridge longitudinal study

Lynne Murray

Lynne Murray

Lynne Murray  

Children with a parent who suffers from depression are at raised risk for the disorder themselves. Relatively little is known about the development of psychological vulnerability to depression through childhood, particularly during infancy and early childhood. This paper describes results from a longitudinal prospective study of the development of children, studied from birth to 16 years, of mothers who experienced postnatal depression; it highlights the roles of the developing mother-child relationship, and of physiological functioning and social relationships in the development of adolescent vulnerability.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma  

This is my first edition of the Journal Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in my new role as its Editor and, as such, I want to share some thoughts about its future direction. First, however, I would like to thank my predecessor, Maureen Marks, for her hard work in developing the Journal over several years and for her very supportive handover. Second, I want to thank David Taylor who is the Guest Editor on this Special Issue on Depression. This series of excellent papers illustrates the rich understanding that results from bringing together diverse perspectives – from psychoanalysis, biology, neuroscience and cognitive science – all of which are relevant to understanding the underlying complexity of both the aetiology and dynamics of depression.

A key question that I have been reflecting on is the purpose of this Journal. Looking back at past issues it is clear that it serves many functions: to communicate within and across our own analytic communities; to support the application of psychoanalysis in the public health sector; to further our learning; and to stimulate thinking and innovate practice. Over many years it has achieved all these things and more besides. It has a long and valued tradition of publishing clinical case studies and practice-based papers from some of our best minds and most talented clinicians, which powerfully illustrate the ongoing relevance of analytic ideas in the NHS. This is a tradition that is very important and that will continue to be preserved, but it should not be privileged over other domains that this Journal also needs to address and engage with if it is to remain relevant to current times.

Those who work in publicly-funded services will know that as a group – diverse though we are – we share a common problem. Moreover, this is not a small problem: psychoanalysis and its applications are under threat. Although we feel this problem most acutely in the NHS, the survival of psychoanalysis in the private sector is closely tied to its survival in the public sector, hence this is a problem that concerns us all whatever settings we work in.

responseSet.title: A case of body image and eating difficulty
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A case of body image and eating difficulty

Liv Darling

Liv Darling

Liv Darling  

body image difficulty, eating disorder, anorexia internal objects, mirroring

This is a clinical discussion of work with a patient with body image and eating difficulties. Thought is given to the use made of the psychotherapeutic setting by the patient, in which limitation of the work and a pleasant elusiveness hides a more active engagement, as well as a secret involvement with more problematic and destructive processes. This constitutes an actualization of the patient's internal object world. Use is made of particular ideas to make sense of this presentation, in particular those from theories about eating disorders.

responseSet.title: Alexis Brook, the good doctor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Alexis Brook, the good doctor

Jennifer Johns

Jennifer Johns

Jennifer Johns  

I must have known Alexis for at least 50 years. I can't remember the exact date that I first met him, but I certainly met him at some of the annual summer parties my parents used to hold in their garden in Putney for the professional staff of the Cassel Hospital, where my father Tom Main was Medical Director for many years. Alexis worked at the Hospital for 15 years from 1956 to 1971, first as a SHMO, then as a consultant, so I would have been a medical student when we first met. I can certainly remember my father's enthusiasm and admiration for this young doctor, and my own impression of him, which was of a quietly warm and calm person, who was prepared to talk to and show interest in a young and anxious medical student like myself, rather in awe of ‘real doctors’. As my own career through medicine developed and I became by turns a general practitioner, a clinical assistant in obstetrics, a part-time child welfare doctor, a family planning specialist and eventually a psychoanalyst, I met Alexis many times during the following years at social occasions both at the Cassel and later when my husband, a child psychiatrist and psychoanalyst, worked at the Child Guidance Training Centre at the Tavistock where so much of Alexis's career was spent. His welcoming smile, personal warmth and continuing interest always made me feel that I was a friend. During these years his family was growing, and then a bit later so was mine, and he always asked what my daughter Becky was doing and he told me about his own children. It was much later, when I was on the Board and Council of the British Psycho-Analytical Society and I was asked to become involved with activities funded by a bequest known as the ‘Inman Fund’, to do with psychosomatic ophthalmology that I actually met with Alexis as a colleague myself, and learned that all my impressions of his thoughtfulness and calm confidence were correct; and were accompanied by his remarkable capacity to assess situations simultaneously on several levels; and also that his penetrating intelligence was used with great generosity and humanity. Typical of Alexis might be a situation in which a committee or working group would be discussing a project or difficulty and trying to puzzle out a solution, a plan of action, a way forward; a situation when each person might have a different point of view, or an axe to grind.

responseSet.title: A Special K retreat: Dynamics which threatened the psychotherapy of an eating-disordered patient
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Special K retreat: Dynamics which threatened the psychotherapy of an eating-disordered patient

Susannah Rose

Susannah Rose

Susannah Rose  

Anorexia, bulimia, threat, retreat, omnipotence, dependency, transference

When considering dynamics which threaten the therapies of patients with eating disorders the first aspect that occurs to me is how much the notion of ‘threat’ is a feature of the life of a person with an eating disorder. Working as a psychotherapist on an in-patient unit for severely ill eating-disordered patients, I am continually reminded how the patients in our unit live with the constant threat of serious physical damage, if not death (although this last is on the whole not felt to be a threat by the patient). At the same time, they are mostly subjected to ferocious internal threats of what will be done to them if they attempt to give up and therefore betray the eating disorder. Patients with eating disorders can be threatening, intimidating and controlling to the people around them; they will vigorously fight anyone who attempts to threaten the perceived safety of their internal system. In addition, they can feel threatened with punishment by the team on the unit, who they feel can increase their food intake or decrease their freedoms at a whim in a controlling and retaliatory manner.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Depression and allied illness in children and adolescents: Basic facts

Anupam Bhardwaj and Ian M. Goodyer

Ian M. Goodyer

Ian M. Goodyer  

depression, children, adolescence, clinical features and aetiology

Unipolar depression is a relatively common psychiatric condition in young people. Clinical signs and symptoms at presentation can vary with age, with children showing different characteristics to adolescents. Depressive episodes before 18 years of age are associated with increased risk of recurrence into adulthood, with some early-onset cases showing a chronic course into adult life. Both recurrent and chronic cases in young adulthood are associated with long-term suffering and impairment. Depressive episodes invariably present with a range of other non-depressive disorders, such that co-morbid presentation is the rule rather than the exception. These early-onset depressions are heterogeneous in their origins and outcomes. Treatment of a depressive episode results in full remission in about two-thirds of cases, but we know little of the effects of treatment on subsequent relapse and recurrence. A better understanding of the causes and the pathophysiology of these disorders would greatly inform treatment decision-making and public health policy.

[[RunningHead]]

The psychology of depression in young adolescents: A psychoanalytic view of origins, inner workings and implications

Margaret Rustin

Margaret Rustin

Margaret Rustin  

Adolescent depression, short-term psychotherapy, adversity, aggression, containment, mental functioning

This paper addresses the phenomenology of childhood depression as understood from the perspective of psychoanalytic child psychotherapy within National Health Service (NHS) practice. Brief time-limited psychotherapy has a promising outcome in working with severely depressed young people when combined with support for parents and careful attention to the child's social and educational context. The currently planned IMPACT study (Improving Mood-Promoting Access to Collaborative Treatment) led by Professor I. Goodyer will provide an opportunity to explore both the effectiveness of short-term therapy for a large cohort of moderate to severely depressed youngsters and also to research models of underlying mental processes and some of the processes of change in psychotherapy (Fonagy, 2009; Rustin, 2009). Child psychotherapy theory and practice is rooted in both psychoanalytic and developmental frames of reference and has been deeply influenced by Bowlby's work. The importance of aggression and particularly aggressive thoughts, both conscious and unconscious, in understanding depression is also fundamental. Two clinical cases are discussed which are typical of those patients seen in NHS child psychotherapy, and of the type of patients who will be seen in the IMPACT trial. They share serious adverse life histories: one with a focus on childhood abuse and the other on neglect and abandonment. The different nature of these traumatic experiences shapes the nature of the depression which can be observed. The importance of attending to the depressive elements of the co-morbid presentation characteristic of Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) patients also needs to be emphasized. Psychotherapy provides an opportunity for containment of unbearable emotion and for the development of mental function which can serve as a bulwark against further breakdown. The conceptualization draws attention to the loss of personal potency which accompanies depression and the consequent risk of life failure in both work and personal spheres.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Change in chronic anorexic patients

Michelle Washington

Michelle Washington

Michelle Washington  

psychic growth, maternal containment, chronic anorexia, behavioural change, recovery

In an attempt to explore the possible factors that contribute to psychic growth, development, change and recovery in anorexic patients, it seems essential to return to the important mental processes that begin during infancy. I am suggesting here that the types of internal conflict and mental dysfunctioning that inhibits the anorexic patient from being able to ‘take-in’ from another, and obstructs the possibility of recovery, can to some extent be explained by a failure in their early containment. By using clinical and theoretical material I will attempt to demonstrate that a recovery from such developmental failure is integral in enabling some degree of psychic and behavioural change in the anorexic patient.

[[RunningHead]]

Thinking about dementia – a psychodynamic understanding of links between early infantile experience and dementia

Lynn Malloy

Lynn Malloy

Lynn Malloy  

dementia, Alzheimer's disease, psychodynamic, containment, carers

This paper explores links between a psychodynamic approach to early infantile experience and how this can underpin an understanding of the states of mind that can be returned to for a person with dementia as the disease progresses. Dementia is more commonly associated with later life, early onset dementia, however, is also a reality. A deeper understanding of the unconscious communications and anxieties in this area and the containment provided by this understanding can help to support more thoughtful work with the person with dementia, their family and the formal support system around them both. The paper uses vignettes from working with a client in his home.

responseSet.title: Depression and attention to two kinds of meaning: A cognitive perspective
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Depression and attention to two kinds of meaning: A cognitive perspective

Philip J. Barnard

Philip J. Barnard

Philip J. Barnard  

depression, meaning, attention, macro-theory, Interacting Cognitive Subsystems

The complexity of a mental disorder such as depression is such that a way of interlinking the neural, mental and interpersonal levels is needed. This paper proposes that a theoretical framework which distinguishes, and relates, macro-theory and micro-theory at these levels can serve this purpose. The ‘Interacting Cognitive Subsystems’ approach to mental architecture is used to show how, via the detailed specification of mental processes and representations, a macro-theory of mental architecture contributes to our understanding of depressed states. In the account advanced by Teasdale and Barnard depressed states are seen as being maintained by an abnormal version of a dynamic dialogue between two qualitatively distinct types of meaning: one is referentially specific, propositional meaning, the other consists of holistic schemata rich in latent content and is called implicational meaning. In depressed states with ruminative and avoidant thought patterns, the mental function of attention is seen as being directed preferentially at propositional meanings. There is a corresponding neglect of attention to implicational meanings. The paper concludes with a brief discussion of how this approach can address transdiagnostic issues and how it may suggest new strategies for therapeutic interventions.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

BOOK REVIEW

Chris Brogan

Chris Brogan

Chris Brogan  

‘You ought to!’. A psychoanalytic study of the superego and conscience

by Bernard Barnett, London, Karnac, 2007, 180 pp., £14.95 (paperback)

Bernard Barnett's book containing six chapters with its pithy title ‘You ought to!’, might create the impression that you could read it on a 2-hour train journey. The compact clarity of discussion would make this enjoyable and achievable but at the end of the journey I think the reader would be hungry to re-read certain passages and chapters. This relatively slim book has considerable depth and scholarship with an effective use of fascinating literary examples.

In the Introduction Dr Barnett defines concepts, with the aid of examples taken from the press, such as the ego ideal, conscience, the ego and the self, remorse, the need for punishment and morality. He distinguishes a narrow definition of Superego, that part of the mind, mainly unconscious, which watches over the ego, with the System Superego which could embrace all of the above. He acknowledges the overlap between these various concepts and ends with a brief synopsis of George Eliot's Daniel Deronda. I found that it was helpful to return to these definitions as I progressed through the book.

The second chapter is dedicated to a very close exposition of Freud's development of the concept of superego, demonstrating Barnett's enormous talents as a teacher of psychoanalysis. I found his description of the later papers, Civilization and its discontents, and Femininity and an outline of psychoanalysis, particularly interesting, especially Freud's references to the cultural influences on the superego and the idea that the superego unites the present with the past.

Barnett's account and development of the superego through childhood and adulthood, attests to his extensive experience as a child analyst and teacher of psychoanalysis. Like the previous chapters, he uses literary examples from Daniel Deronda and James Joyce's Portrait of the artist as a young man. For instance, in Daniel Deronda he illustrates a particular aspect of female Oedipal development in Gwendolen, who is struggling with her identification with a rather weak and childlike mother. Daniel is a father figure, offering Gwendolen advice and support after the death of her uncaring, cold husband. He helps her with her partly conscious guilt and enables her to disentangle herself from her mother and to become more empathic towards her.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Maureen Marks

Maureen Marks

Maureen Marks  

This is my last issue as Editor of the Journal. The incoming Editor is Alessandra Lemma, who will be assisted by Tom Pennybacker. Alessandra is a psychoanalyst and clinical psychologist with many years of experience working in the NHS. She has also published several books on psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy. The Journal will be in good hands.

The Association for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in the National Health Service (the APP) was formally established in 1981. It may be timely to remind readers of the aims of the APP, and hence the Journal's aims. These are detailed in the APP's constitution and are as follows:

2 (a) The objects for which the Association is established are for the advancement of the science and skill of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy in the Public Health Sector, and the teaching thereof.

2 (b) In furtherance of the objects of the Association it may:

 (vi) Reproduce, publish and distribute in any language and in any part of the world works in any medium on Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy. In particular, the journal established in 1984 entitled PSYCHOANALYTIC PSYCHOTHERAPY shall be the official journal of the APP. The editor shall be a full member of the APP and his/her appointment shall be ratified by Council. The editor shall automatically become a member of Council. The editor shall at his/her own discretion appoint an editorial board of psychoanalysts and intensively trained psychoanalytic psychotherapists of appropriate experience. PSYCHOANALYTIC PSYCHOTHERAPY shall be a scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed papers on psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy and their applications, with special emphasis on work in the public sector relevant to the aims and interest of the APP. The editor, taking the advice also of editorial board members, shall be the final arbiter of the published contents of the journal.

I think that the content of this issue represents what the Journal, and the APP, are about. The papers are from authors with a range of experience in psychoanalytic and psychiatric training and practice, and the content of their papers is also diverse: working with people who have dementia, have experienced child sexual abuse, who are suicidal, and thinking about whistleblowers, in the NHS.

responseSet.title: Moving psychotherapy into community settings: Alexis Brook's life's work
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Moving psychotherapy into community settings: Alexis Brook's life's work

Mannie Sher

Mannie Sher

Mannie Sher  

For 6 months after I joined the Adult Department of the Tavistock Clinic in October 1971, Dr Alexis Brook was a member of the Department whom I knew only vaguely. However, I gradually warmed to him as we drank our coffees in the staff common room. He was the Head of another Intake Team down the corridor, but his friendly, warm chats in the common room about human nature, politics, a lively interest in ordinary matters, like his strong objections to the type and colour of roof that was being installed on the houses across the street, made me wish to work with him. I must have mentioned this to my supervisors, Judith Stephens and Jane Temperley, who worked in Alexis Brook's Intake team, because some time later Alexis asked me to join the Community Unit. This Unit was important for the Adult Department because it carried psychoanalytic psychotherapy to the outside world as an outward-looking community-based approach to mental health problems. This was Alexis's forte – introducing psychodynamic work into community-based clinics and departments, and in the case of this particular workshop, to general practice. Alexis already had a wide reputation for his methods of enlarging psychological understanding of patients and relationships between patients and their professional carers in ophthalmology, general psychiatry and social work.

Alexis explained the purpose of the Unit as part of a project of psychotherapy attachments to general practice. These attachments, he said, were different from normal secondments; they were experimental. The project attempted to answer the question: ‘what contribution could a psychotherapist, spending three hours a week in a general practice, make to the mental health of patients attending that practice?’. It was clear, he often said, that the psychotherapist would not simply transport a method of work from the psychotherapy consulting room to general practice. A new role and a new method of work would have to be devised to optimize the opportunities presented by the presence in the practice of a psychotherapist.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Book Reviews

Simon Archer

Simon Archer

Simon Archer  

Sensuality and Sexuality across the Divide of Shame

Joseph D. Lichtenberg, (2008). The Analytic Press, Psychoanalytic Inquiry Book Series, New York, pp. 160, ISBN: 978-0-88163-475-1, Paperback £21-99

Shame and Sexuality – Psychoanalysis and Visual Culture

Claire Pajaczkowska and Ivan Ward, Routledge, Sussex and New York, pp. 242, ISBN 978-0-415-42011-9, Paperback, £19-99

These two books are a useful addition to a subject neglected within psychoanalysis, particularly in Britain. Shame is a topic that has attracted more interest in the U.S.A. and France (Sartre and Lacan being a powerful influence). When I first took an interest in shame in the 1980's (I was working with young criminals who seemed to be entirely occupied with issues of shame and ‘saving face’), a search revealed only a handful of psychoanalytic papers on shame. This is no longer the case but it is still rare to find major emotional disturbance attributed to chronic shame. It is quite possible to make a case that Oedipus does not have an Oedipus complex (he bypasses it) and instead has a shame-driven personality disorder. Shame has been extensively studied outside of psychoanalysis by counsellors, anthropologists, neuroscientists and psychologists. The neglect within psychoanalysis is most likely due to several factors: Freud made no systematic study of it (but a search of the Standard Edition reveals that the word ‘shame’ occurs much more often than ‘guilt’). Freud's self-analysis was unlikely to reveal shame because it is activated by the presence of another person. Psychoanalytic orthodoxy following Freud emphasises guilt and its vicissitudes. Western, Judaeo-Christian culture also emphasises guilt (which allows the masking of more painful shame).

Shame has begun to emerge from the shadows as it becomes recognised as powerful in its contribution to both the constructive and destructive aspects of humanity. Both of these books indirectly pose the question ‘what is useful and what needs to be revised in the Freudian system in order for the analysis of shame to find a coherent place within psychoanalysis?’

Tributes and contributions from the floor

John Lennard-Jones (formerly Consultant Gastroenterologist at St. Mark's) writes:

St Mark's is a small, specialist hospital dealing with intestinal and colorectal disorders. It has a particular reputation for the surgical skill of its staff. Patients with inflammatory conditions, such as ulcerative colitis or Crohn's disease, often benefit from a team approach by physician, surgeon and other specialities. Some patients suffer severe symptoms but without evidence of structural or metabolic disease, and their symptoms appear due to disorders of function which are hard to define.

The treatment of patients with emotional problems at St Mark's took on a new direction and effectiveness when Lady Juliet Bingley was appointed medical social worker in 1973. She had a flair for helping patients to cope with long-term disabling illness and for affirming the personality of patients with emotional problems, often dating from damaging factors in their background. She felt that she needed consultant help in certain cases. In 1979 an informal good-will arrangement was made with Dr Peter Schoenberg, consultant psychotherapist at University College Hospital, who visited St Mark's, voluntarily and unpaid, on a weekly basis to see patients. When he had to stop these visits due to other commitments, Alexis Brook accepted an invitation in 1985 to fulfil the same role.

Alexis had just retired from his consultant post at the Tavistock Clinic and he worked part-time for 9 years at St Mark's on an informal basis as an Honorary Consultant Psychiatrist. Some temporary financial support was obtained initially from the King Edward Hospital Fund and later from the St Mark's Research Foundation after which his work was entirely voluntary. His contribution to the clinical work, his teaching and his attractive personality did much to establish the need for a Department of Psychological Medicine at the hospital, now regarded as an essential component of its work.

Alexis was a gentle, perceptive, person with a quiet manner and dis-arming smile. He had a particular skill in defining the root of a person's emotional difficulty. As in general practice, he saw a patient once or twice, very occasionally more, for approximately an hour on each occasion. He did not attempt to instigate treatment himself. These consultations led to an informal weekly seminar held on Thursday mornings in the Chapel at St Mark's, then in the City Road, attended by Lady Bingley, and by members of the medical and nursing staff.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Breaking up blues

Paul Foster

Paul Foster

Paul Foster  

Denise Cullington, London, Routledge, 2008, 282 pp., £40.00 (hardback), £8.99 (paperback)

Why a book on divorce? Cullington, a psychoanalyst and clinical psychologist, has first-hand experience of divorce and subsequently wrote Breaking up blues.

Her account is scattered with patients' stories, drawings, and works of fiction. Her style is humane, never patronizing, and she ably puts the most difficult experiences into words:

You find yourself caught up in the emotional rip tides that pull you off your feet; suck you dangerously far out to sea; throw you over and over in the waves before smashing you on the sand or the rocks (p. 4).

Her self-help manual in six parts charts a course through this unfamiliar terrain.

Part One describes weighing up whether to leave, and how ongoing stresses including an affair provoke difficulties, which may result in parting. There is no right answer to how and when to leave, the outcome may be unpredictable and there will inevitably be regrets. Divorce is hard work requiring compromise and understanding, more a marathon than a sprint.

Part Two describes the losses of home, security, family role and structure, contact with children and mutual friends. These can be devastating, particularly for men, who often close down emotionally. The internal losses of identity, ‘innocence’ and self-worth are discussed and the anxiety resulting from the disrupted attachment. There is a chapter on the rage of loss which examines the hatred (25% of children witnessed physical violence at the time of marital break up), the desire for vengeance and punishment:

although you may know, logically, that you need to resist the pull into this exhilarating maw of outrage, accusation and counteraccusation, it can happen so quickly and unexpectedly that it can be hard to resist, and hard to find your way back from (p. 4).

Her helpful conclusion is ‘the best means of revenge is to live a full life yourself' (p. 66).

Cullington describes how the adversarial legal system polarizes positions. A ‘fair share’ in terms of access to children is impossible. In Florida there are assumed automatic rights of paternal access to children. Britain lags behind in this.

[[RunningHead]]

Neuroanalysis: Bridging the gap between neuroscience, psychoanalysis and psychiatry

Kevin Healy

Kevin Healy

Kevin Healy  

Avi Peled, London, Routledge, 2008, 150 pp., £20.99 (paperback), £55 (hardback)

The anxious brain: The neurobiological basis of anxiety disorders and how to effectively treat them

Margaret Wehrenberg and Stephen Prinz, London, W.W. Norton and Co., 274 pp, £22.99 (hardback)

I have become increasingly interested in the subject of brain development and functioning over the past 8 years. This interest stems from my own continuing development, from my observations of my adolescent children in their transitional developments, from my work as a clinician with individuals with personality disorder and with emerging personality disorder, and from my work as a psychotherapist in attempting to understand more fully how treatment can be most effective. During this time I have read extensively, including books and articles written by Cozolino (2006), Winston (2003), Greenfield (2000), Blakemore, and Frith (2005), Folensbee (2007), Doidge (2008), Shore (1994), Fonagy (2006), Le Doux (2002), and Kandel (2006). The relevant works of all of these individuals will appear readily when ‘Googled’. Indeed ‘Googling’ brain development will lead you to all sorts of interesting sites. A particularly good one on child development is from a group in America called ‘Zero to 3’. You Tube has many helpful presentations from the above authors and others about their work on the brain.

I was therefore very pleased to see that the book by Peled on Neuroanalysis was available for reviewing in December's Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy. Julian Stern, Book Review Editor, kindly sent me the second book on The Anxious Brain when I contacted him with a request to review this book. Initially I imagined this second book might prove to be more medical in its orientation, and perhaps less interesting.

I have spent the last 2 months reading, re-reading, reading around and thinking about the contents of both of these books. My expectations were not met. Overall I found the short book on neuroanalysis to be a difficult book to read. I suspect English is not Avi Peled's natural tongue. Some of the terminology used is difficult to follow and does not readily link, in my mind, to what is being described. On the other hand, the book on the anxious brain I found to be clear, coherent and informative. I will outline more of the fascinating content of both books below.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychotherapy, medicine and the body: A tribute to the work of Alexis Brook

Andrew Elder , Sarah Majid , Julian Stern and Guest Editors

Guest Editors

Guest Editors  

This special edition of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy gathers together papers and contributions from the Alexis Brook Memorial Conference, held at the Tavistock Centre, London in October 2008. Alexis Brook was an extraordinary man, both personally and professionally, whose innovative work touched the lives of many and continues to inspire clinicians to this day. In organizing this one-day conference our aim was 2-fold. We wanted to celebrate and honour the breadth and depth of his work as a fitting memorial to his life, and were delighted that many of his family and former colleagues were able to attend. We also wanted to share his creativity and wisdom in a stimulating academic event, and let his insights and enthusiasm inspire a new generation of clinicians who might never have met or heard of him – or of psychotherapy. We therefore invited speakers for the second half of the conference to present current thinking and clinical work with psychosomatic disorders, building on Brook's pioneering development of this field. We aimed to attract delegates from a range of specialities and disciplines reflecting the diversity of his influence. Delegates did indeed come from an extraordinary range of professional disciplines, reflecting his links with ophthalmology, general practice, coloproctology and gastroenterology, psychoanalysis and psychiatry, occupational health and nursing. Seldom indeed have professors of surgery and medicine, esteemed nurses, retired and current GPs and many others, spoken at a public conference at the Tavistock Clinic!

We are fortunate in being able to publish most of the conference papers in this special edition, reflecting the important themes as they developed through the day. We began with an introduction by Nick Temple, former Chief Executive of the Tavistock and Portman NHS Trust, who had supported setting up the conference in recognition of Brook's invaluable contribution to the work of the Tavistock as consultant psychiatrist and Chair. Temple outlined significant areas of Brook's work in general practice, ophthalmology and gastroenterology. He drew particular attention to Brook's unassuming and warm style that both won the confidence of medical and surgical staff, and enabled them to hear and use his psychoanalytic thinking to understand their patients and explore their own reactions without becoming defensive.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Building on the work of Alexis Brook: Further thoughts about brief psychotherapy in primary care

Andrew Elder

Andrew Elder

Andrew Elder  

This paper describes the pioneering work of Alexis Brook in exploring the value of psychotherapists working alongside GPs in their practices. It starts by describing two seminal papers published by Brook in 1967 and by Brook and Temperley in 1976, which illustrate his emphasis on the importance of a collaborative approach between professionals, and on an understanding of the natural advantages of general practices for mental health work. The author then describes briefly some of the more recent developments in counselling and psychological therapies in primary care. This perspective allows the differences to be drawn between the current approach in which general practitioners are encouraged to relinquish their role in mental illness, and the approach adopted earlier by Alexis Brook and his co-workers where psychotherapeutic skills are used to help professionals in practices develop their own resources. The paper concludes by arguing for a much-needed re-appraisal of the theory and practice of brief psychotherapy in primary care, one which would take full account of the psychological characteristics of the setting and build on the earlier insights and approach of Alexis Brook and his colleagues.

responseSet.title: Around the table
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Around the table

Carol Bowyer

Carol Bowyer

Carol Bowyer  

food, eating disorders, eating behaviour, feelings

The dynamics around the dining room table on an Eating Disorders Unit, the patients' relationship with food and eating, together with those of the staff feeding them can all be seen as a reflection of the dynamics on the Unit at large and may externalize an individual's internal world. The three, hour-long meals each day can be some of the most difficult hours on an Eating Disorders Unit, anxiety provoking and stressful. Within this paper I describe the behaviours observed around food and think about the feelings engendered at the table as experienced by both patients and staff members. Furthermore, as a single-handed dietitian, working within a multidisciplinary team, I also consider my professional role on the Unit and within the feeding process. I shall draw upon psychoanalytic literature to explore the beginnings of the relationship between mother and infant that can be examined through the vital feeding relationship. When a person falls into the trap of an eating disorder, it frequently brings sufferer and parent, generally the mother, back into a relationship concentrating on food and feeding but in a far more fraught and painful manner. All names have been changed and some background details have been altered or omitted to protect identities.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Suicidality as an expression of ‘Psychic retreats’: Some clinical and theoretical aspects1

Heinz Weiß

Heinz Weiß

Heinz Weiß  

The paper aims to show that the theory of ‘Psychic Retreats’ can offer a clinically helpful framework for understanding manifest and latent suicidal tendencies. A psychic retreat is often clung to when the contact with reality (internal or external) becomes too frightening or would confront the patient with unbearable psychic pain. In such a situation a retreat may serve as a temporary refuge; but in its chronic form it leads to an impasse which can obstruct any possibility of psychic development and often reflects a pathological organization of the personality. Originally aimed at binding and neutralizing destructive impulses, the retreat may then become itself an expression of chronic and tormenting destructiveness. Using clinical material the paper illustrates how suicidal fantasies can create an impasse which serves to control the analyst by making use of perverse mechanisms and specific forms of projective identification. On the other hand, a psychic retreat may also serve as a masquerade to cover up suicidal tendencies. In the first form the underlying cruelty is displayed more openly while in the second it is disguised by romanticization and idealization. Both forms of a psychic retreat confront the therapist with considerable technical difficulties and make it necessary to carefully register and interpret the subtle enactments of very pathological object relations within the transference relationship.

responseSet.title: Inaccessibility in eating disorders
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Inaccessibility in eating disorders

Alexandra Willner

Alexandra Willner

Alexandra Willner  

inaccessibility, eating disorders, anorexia, bulimia

This paper details some different manifestations of inaccessibility encountered in working with eating disorders. Using clinical material relating to anorexia and bulimia, the destructive and defensive measures that make it so difficult to reach, understand and change such pathologies are explored. The main hypothesis is that the degree of inaccessibility is a salient prognostic indicator and marker of the severity of the eating disorder. The role and meaning of extreme, pervasive and rigid inaccessibility is considered along with psychic positions of greater porosity that offer more hope, optimism and ‘movement towards life’. Finally, the therapeutic implications are examined in attempts to work with these difficult to engage and often refractory conditions, both at the individual and institutional level.

[[RunningHead]]

Postscript

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy  

It is never an easy task to summarize a set of papers when each paper would deserve the 2,000 words that the discussants have available, and each contribution triggers thoughts and associations sufficient to match each of the papers in terms of length, even if not in terms of quality. David Taylor's instruction was to provide a “real discussion” which elaborates the findings presented, and brings together some of the common themes to emerge from the papers.

The first point to make about this collection of excellent contributions concerns the duality already identified by Taylor in his preface. There appear to be numerous dichotomies that still characterize the field as represented in these papers which indicate that coherent integration may be more aspirational than a realistic goal. The first of these is developmental: childhood and adolescent depression is treated and studied separately from its adult counterpart in most of these papers as elsewhere in the literature, yet we know the two to be different faces of the same coin (e.g. Dunn & Goodyer, 2006). The separation of this collection of papers into child and adult sections is inevitable given the current state of the literature but seems to us to mark a regrettable reluctance on the part of our community to adopt a socio-neuro-developmental view of the disorder – reluctance probably born more from the constraints of service organisation than the limitations of current scientific knowledge.

A similar regrettable dichotomy emerges from laboratory versus clinical encounter oriented papers. Rustin and Taylor do remarkable work in presenting to us the dilemmas facing the clinician confronted with adolescents and adults whose subjective experience can be so profoundly negative that death is a preferable alternative. The interiority of the clinical account is only partially met by the exteriority of the laboratory science. Even Murray's sensitive account of the babies of depressed mothers, or Hill's beautifully crafted, elaborate model of the consequences of sexual abuse, take up some, but by no means all, of the complexities which the interiority accounts present.

[[RunningHead]]

Book Reviews

James Johnston

James Johnston

James Johnston  

Psychic assaults and frightened clinicians: Countertransference in forensic settings

John Gordon and Gabriel Kirtchuk ., Forensic Psychotherapy Monograph Series, editor Brett Kahr, London, Karnac Books, 2008, 152 pp., £19.99 (paperback)

There are these two young fish swimming along, and they happen to meet an older fish swimming the other way, who nods at them and says, ‘Morning, boys, how's the water?’. And the two young fish swim on for a bit, and then eventually one of them looks over at the other and goes, ‘What the hell is water?’. Written by David Foster Wallace (2005), this fish story was quoted by Priscilla Roth at a recent psychoanalytic conference (2008). She used it to describe how we can be totally unaware of the shared emotional element in which we are swimming with our patients.

When the fish are at the bottom of a boat, flapping and gasping for air, having been caught by the fisherman, then they know what water is. This comment was made by John Steiner (2008) at the same conference, considering the challenge to the patient of being out of their necessary defensive element as analogous to being a fish out of water. Being caught is the legal reason that the forensic patient becomes a forensic patient. The contaminated water in which they swim predates the sterile hook called the ‘index offence’ on which they were caught. The developmental beginnings of the criminal act remain a pathological buoyancy, nameless, silent and pervasive.

This book is clear about the nature of the water in which the forensic patient and the professionals who work with them swim. It is also clear that in these murky polluted depths people are reluctant to look at what is in the water in which they are swimming. To extend the water analogy, the forensic institution in which they are incarcerated is like a river made toxic by the poisoned chemicals of psychosis, personality disorder, trauma, perversion, an ever-present risk of violence and the obliteration of the oxygen of meaning. In forensic settings the inner world cannot be allowed the ‘oxygen of publicity’:

To experience strong emotional reactions to patients, let alone to do so regularly or to discuss them in staff meetings, is equivalent to professional suicide (p. 1).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Developmental perspectives on adult depression

Jonathan Hill

Jonathan Hill

Jonathan Hill  

depression, sexual abuse, emotion regulation, gender

This paper identifies how developmental approaches can contribute to our understanding of depression. First, our understanding of the role of childhood experience, and its interaction with genetic variation, is considered. The paper then examines how the consequences of different kinds of maltreatment in the course of development point to contrasting mechanisms linking adversity to depression. This strengthens our understanding of the heterogeneity which is encountered in depression. Developmental research strongly supports the view that depression is a complex disorder both in terms of its character and origins, and that it may be arrived at along multiple pathways.

responseSet.title: Depression in adults: Some basic facts
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Depression in adults: Some basic facts

Daniel McQueen

Daniel McQueen

Daniel McQueen  

Depression, dsythymia, melancholia, aetiology, epidemiology, classification

This paper reviews some of the essential facts about depression in adults. The term can refer to a symptom, a syndrome or a diagnosis. As a symptom, it occurs in many mental disorders and physical diseases. The boundaries of depression are reviewed: depression is biologically distinct from bipolar affective disorder, but it overlaps with the other common neurotic conditions; the spectrum of depressive disorders, melancholia, depressive adjustment reactions and dysthymia is described. Categorical classifications of depression have many problems, but are currently necessary. The epidemiology of depression in adulthood is presented with reference to old age, after childbirth, its prognosis and long-term disability and the limitations of short-term treatments. The immediate causes of depression include complex interactions between life events and their personal meaning influenced by personality factors and neuroticism. Ethological perspectives indicate that depression may have both adaptive and maladaptive aspects.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The dance of dissent: Managing conflict in healthcare organizations

Sameer P. Sarkar

Sameer P. Sarkar

Sameer P. Sarkar   Dr Sarkar is a Consultant in Psychiatry and Forensic Psychiatry in full-time private practice. He worked in one of England's three maximum-security hospitals as a consultant for 5 years. Prior to being appointed a consultant, Dr Sarkar trained in Forensic Psychiatry at the Institute of Psychiatry at the Maudsley Hospital, London. He had extensive experience in group and individual psychotherapy during his psychiatric training in Philadelphia, USA. He has also read law at Harvard University and Northumbria University and writes mostly on issues of law and ethics. Dr Sarkar serves on the editorial boards of the Journal of American Academy of Psychiatry and the Law, and Journal of Ethics in Mental Health. He is an active member of the Royal College of Psychiatrists and has been serving on its ethics committee since 2001.
scapegoating, dissent, groupthink, whistleblowing, organizational dynamics

In this paper, I will suggest that healthcare institutions are intolerant of dissent, and use scapegoating as a defence. When dissent involves criticism of the organization, including whistle-blowing activities, the dissenter is likely to be punished and excluded. I will suggest that this process represents a paranoid defence in which the organization acts as an antigroup.

responseSet.title: Guest Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Guest Editorial

David Taylor

David Taylor

David Taylor  

This issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, devoted solely to depressive disorders, puts the findings of neuroscience, behavioural genetics, developmental research, cognitive science, psychiatry and psychoanalytic approaches between the same two covers.

Readers of this journal might have a sense of why we choose to focus upon depression in this way. The significant health–economic burden associated with depression in the developed world has been increasingly recognized. The extension of evidence-based medicine into the mental health field has created a demand for evidence which has been largely sourced by turning to randomized controlled trials, predominantly of short-term cognitive therapies. However, many of us would argue that their findings have been over-estimated. The scoping criteria used by the National Institute for Clinical Excellence (NICE) adult depression guidelines rest on an assumption that ‘depression’ can be meaningfully separated out from the general category of common mental disorder (which used to be called mixed neurotic disorders). These criteria determined which studies were admitted and which not: most psychodynamic therapy studies were ruled out. In the UK, as we know, this set of factors was then used to argue for a major increase in the provision of short-term, predominantly cognitive, psychological therapies for the treatment of depression.

In truth, significant though the consequences of the resulting re-organization of the mental health provisions may prove to be, they are not the main reason for the interdisciplinary approach adopted for this issue. Rather, it is that scientific progress is making it unavoidable. Advances in neuroscience, genetics and developmental research have led to so many new findings that the theories offered by any isolated discipline are under strain. It is important to recollect that the coming paradigm shift applies as much to 19th, 20th and early 21st century models of psychiatry, or to the current theories of neuropathology or cognitive science, as it does to those of psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Mind Works: Technique and Creativity in Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mind Works: Technique and Creativity in Psychoanalysis

Colin Thomas

Colin Thomas

Colin Thomas  

Antonino Ferro, Routledge, 2009, ISBN 978-0-415-42992-4

Antonino Ferro's book is an articulate and creative exploration of Bion's clinical and theoretical insights and represents a significant development of post-Bion theory, in addition developing many subtle points about the technique of modern psychoanalysis.

Ferro amasses an impressive range of vignettes and clinical samples from his own work, and that of others, to draw out the spirit of Bion's clinical approach. He starts this in a subtle way, placing emphasis on scenes and situations brought to analysis, which eventually compel the attention of the reader. This approach produces emotions in him suggestive of the countertransference experiences Ferro wishes to emphasise in understanding disturbance. Ferro uses these building blocks to form an argument about the clinical importance of the states of mind of the analyst in following states of mind in his patients.

The initial chapter is somewhat difficult to read one's way into until Ferro becomes clearer and more supported by his comprehensive theoretical structure, developed in the central and later chapters.

As an analyst influenced by Bion's work, and the wider work on enactment and counter-transference, I found Ferro's approach a compelling way to convince us of the depth of his own clinical convictions. These are formed through experience, many years ‘simmering’ in the crucible of his own imaginative mental states in being affected by his patients' main concerns, many of them very deep and disturbing. He conveys his conviction in a sensitive way, however, as one feels constantly that the patients' central unconscious dilemmas are to be brought into the foreground as he reviews his own emotional states in relation to them. He further emphasises the need to work, where possible, at an adequate level of contact with the patient's experience of the container, so that the emotional states rather than an intellectual review of them are kept paramount. The overall sense is of a very experienced and impressive analyst at work listening carefully to the listening and receptive capacities of his patients and to his own (Faimberg 2005).

[[RunningHead]]

Keeping the gut in mind

Julian Stern

Julian Stern

Julian Stern  

This paper reviews some of the ways in which Alexis Brook pioneered a truly psychosomatic approach at St Mark's Hospital (SMH), where he worked from 1986 for 9 years (and where the author succeeded him in 1995). This is a highly specialized hospital for disorders of the lower gastrointestinal tract, attracting referrals from throughout the UK and beyond.

Aspects of his work at SMH are described, mainly through the use of his papers, both published and unpublished. The author shows how Brook's legacy lives on, most concretely through a dedicated Psychological Medicine Unit at SMH, which builds on his work, and continues to influence numerous physicians, surgeons, dieticians and nurses who work there, along with many of the patients treated at this hospital.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sorrow, shame, and self-esteem: Perception of self and others in groups for women survivors of child sexual abuse

Paola Valerio and Georgia Lepper

Georgia Lepper

Georgia Lepper  

survivors, incest, group therapy, outcomes, process

Despite evidence that exists about the severity of impact of child sexual abuse (CSA), less has been published about treatment options. Much CSA is enshrouded in secrecy in the context of dysfunctional family dynamics, therefore group work is often recommended as a method of treatment. Although long- and short-term groups are seen as being equally effective, there are few published studies of long-term analytic groups for survivors of CSA. This study was designed to analyse the process of one long-term group. A case is made for using Core Conflictual Relationship Theme (CCRT), a method designed to analyse individual therapy, with reference to group theory. Shifts in self-perception and in relation to others are analysed from the beginning to the end of therapy.

responseSet.title: Time, Space and Phantasy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Time, Space and Phantasy

Chantal Lechartier Atlan

Chantal Lechartier Atlan

Chantal Lechartier Atlan  

Rosine Jozef Perelberg, London, Routledge, 2008, 221 pp., £21.99

I read Time, Space and Phantasy on two different levels: the first as an invitation to travel with Rosine Perelberg through her personal time and space, from Brazil to London, eventually Paris, from her first interest in anthropology to psychoanalysis via the London School of Economics, the Tavistock Clinic, Maudsley Hospital and the British Institute of Psychoanalysis. All these different spaces create a particularly complex and rich inner experience and put her in a privileged position to write a remarkable book. The wide range of her interests is conveyed by a glance at the author's references: 16 full pages ranging from Karl Abraham to C. Yorke, including English, French, American and South American psychoanalytical authors but also anthropologists, sociologists and philosophers.

The second level was a fruitful journey through metapsychological and clinical material thanks to her capacity to synthesize clearly numerous lines of thought and to enrich psychoanalytic contemporary reflection. In my opinion, despite the three nouns in the title, it is the notions of time and temporality that are the core of her book. Space and phantasy, none the less, unfold in time.

The first chapter gives us an overall view of Freud's multi-dimensional model of temporality from the developmental model to the structural one, from the timelessness of the unconscious to the constitution of the ego ‘whose different timings are inaugurated by repression’ (p. 26). To English readers, chapters 2 and 7 will probably appear as the most original as they reflect on the notion of ‘après coup’. Rosine Perelberg explains why she prefers to keep the term of après coup rather than deferred action which, in her opinion, does not fully account for the reversibility of psychic life as it unfolds in phantasies, dreams and symptoms. Après coup, as it appears in Freud's writings, is linked, of course, to the concept of the unconscious and to repetition which account for the constant reshaping of memories and phantasies throughout life and/or analysis. She goes one metapsychological step further in chapter 7, with her distinction between ‘descriptive après coup’ and ‘dynamic après coup’ in the model of the distinction between descriptive unconscious and dynamic unconscious. She relates the former to clinical experience and the latter to metapsychology; and as Rosine Perelberg is no stranger to the ‘foreign tribe’ of French psychoanalysts it is at the Colloque de Deauville that she recently (in October 2006) developed those ideas paradoxically centered on the controversial discussions which never mention the après coup.

responseSet.title: Building out into the dark: Theory and observation in science and psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Building out into the dark: Theory and observation in science and psychoanalysis

Karl Figlio

Karl Figlio

Karl Figlio  

Robert Caper, London/New York: Routledge, 2009, ix +107 pp., £50 (hardback), £18.99 (paperback)

From Freud's time to the present, psychoanalysis has combined a form of therapy with a research methodology and a body of knowledge. The indissoluble link between treatment and knowledge keeps the emphasis of psychoanalytic work on the emotional liveliness of the moment. It marginalizes a separate body of theory or knowledge, treating it as a defence against the immediate emotional contact between the patient and analyst and between the patient's own conscious and unconscious minds. Here is its unique strength and its unique weakness. On the one hand, psychoanalysis aims to restore a psychic harmony sufficient for feeling emotionally alive, based on mental flexibility, judgement and satisfaction in the actual world; on the other hand, it cannot readily call on an agreed body of theory or on public evidence to support it, or on practices that follow from its theories.

This tension between practice and knowledge easily fractures into two views of psychoanalysis (as either art or science) and two corresponding views on how to validate it (either by appeal to an art-like aspect of the process, such as empathy, or by appeal to scientific verification). These dichotomies affect the training, socialization, qualification and collegial recognition of a psychoanalyst. Typically, a good psychoanalyst can demonstrate to colleagues in his or her school an ability to maintain a psychoanalytic process, but not the scientific validity of psychoanalytic theories and practices; indeed, there is typically an indifference to scientific criteria. None the less, an imperative, from inside and outside the field, to demonstrate the place of psychoanalysis among the sciences has been a thorn in its side since Freud.

A typical reply to the scientific challenge is to retreat into claiming that psychoanalysis isn't that sort of beast. It is, for example, not a science, but a hermeneutic process. In this book, Caper rejects such a view, and argues, borrowing an expression of Freud's, for an imaginative venturing ‘out into the dark’ of unexpected observations. He addresses the issue of what science is, in order to work out what psychoanalysis, by contrast, is. He says that science deals with large numbers of identical events; with individual cases as instances of general laws; with verification in controlled experiments and with the consolidation of established observations into theories that, in turn, open opportunities for new observations, generating a systematic spiral of the growth of knowledge.

responseSet.title: Spontaneity – a psychoanalytic enquiry
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Spontaneity – a psychoanalytic enquiry

James Rose

James Rose

James Rose  

Gemma Corradi Fiumara, London, Routledge, 2009, £22.99 (hardback)

Gemma Fiumara is a former professor of philosophy at the Third University of Rome and a training analyst with the Italian Psychoanalytic Society. She is thus well placed to explore the notion of spontaneity – perhaps one of the most subjective of phenomena. The significance of our spontaneity is that it reflects us in our moments of being most truly ourselves. A thesis that runs through the book and stated in the last line is that ‘both as individuals and as a species we have more to gain from accepted vulnerability than from illusory power’.

This thesis perhaps captures the essence of this book that might be entitled ‘Spontaneity and its discontents’. The final chapter on self-formation and self de-creation puts forward a position saying that as we develop through our lives, we must be in a process of adapting to the exigencies of the external world but also discarding those processes and structures that may have suited us in the past but which are no longer relevant or necessary. Fiumara thus looks at the nature of the human subject and its predicament through the lens of spontaneity.

Right at the beginning, Fiumara sets out her challenge to psychoanalysts. ‘If psychoanalysis is not a humble self-questioning venture that allows itself to be constantly challenged by the enigmatic aspects of mental life – as, for instance, spontaneity – then it may well metamorphosize into a most hubristic form of thought’. The nature of ‘spontaneity’ then is a basic question of psychoanalytical research – as is the notion of subjective agency – and its challenge is that ‘the questioner must be included in the question’, in the sense that ‘the immense complexity of the inquirer cannot be excluded’.

With this daunting proviso in mind, Fiumara launches into her review of the ‘enemies’ of spontaneity. The reviewer must be careful in his language here of not falling into an unfortunate splitting in his use of the word ‘enemies’. For, if in being spontaneous we experience a moment of being truly ourselves, then it follows – or seems to – that the manner in which we are aware of others and the requirements we believe they place upon us jeopardizes our capacity for spontaneity. A powerful enemy of spontaneity thus appears to be our narcissism; thought of as being concerned essentially with how we believe we appear to others. The question is then how do we accept our vulnerability and how do we judge when we are vulnerable for neurotic or psychotic reasons and when for realistic ones? We can get a taste here of the philosopher in Fiumara as she takes us through the various impacts on the capacity for spontaneity of, for example, a sense of entitlement, forgiveness, and sympathy and empathy.
